data.title: Exxon Mobil: America's Invasion Could Be Your Persuasion To Invest In This Dividend Aristocrat
data.description:
Introduction

With the U.S. capture of the Venezuelan President, Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) jumped on the news, likely due to the country's oil reserves. And President Trump mentioned that oil companies will invest heavily to rebuild the country.

He has also stated that the U.S. will maintain control until a safe, proper, and judicious transition happens.

When that will be? No one knows. However, the Energy (XLRE) sector will likely do well since Trump stated the capture of the Venezuelan President will allow American based companies to drill in the country's long neglected oil reserves.

Going forward, companies like XOM should see solid growth as a result. Currently, peer Chevron (CVX) is the only one with operations in the country.

And if you're a long-term dividend investor, then XOM could potentially be attractive as this could lead to higher growth. Thus, positively impacting their share price.

In this article, I discuss Exxon Mobil's fundamentals, dividend history, and why this Dividend Aristocrat could be an attractive investment for long-term, income-oriented investors.

Previous Thesis

It's been more than 2 years since I last covered Exxon Mobil, last assigning the stock a buy rating back in October 2023. Since, the stock is up, but has significantly lagged the performance of the S&P (SP500).

XOM is up close to 19% in the past 27 months, while the index is up close to 67%. Despite their underperformance, I believed XOM was attractive due to their strong fundamentals and upside to their price target of $128. Currently, the stock sits slightly below this at $125.36 a share.

Latest Earnings

During their latest Q3 earnings this past October, XOM delivered what some may view as a disappointing earnings report. Their top and bottom lines fell year-over-year due to lower crude-oil prices, suppressed margins, inflation, and lower volumes.

Through the first 3 quarters, both revenue and earnings per share were down from the prior year. Revenue totaled $249.9 billion, down from $266.2 billion, while their bottom line fell double-digits to $5.16 from $6.12.

However, the oil giant saw some positives during the quarter. Guyana, which now accounts for 2/3 of the company's workforce, saw more than 700,000 oil barrels per day.

XOM also launched a new development in Guyana, Yellowtail, which contributed to the record 700,000 barrels per day. Oil & gas production increased more than 139,000 barrels a day.

The Permian Basin saw a production record close to 1.7 million barrels per day. Additionally, XOM acquired 80 acres of land, increasing drilling capacity, leading to potentially higher returns going forward.

Although some projects like Yellowtail have already come online, XOM has a slew of projects in the coming years that will likely drive solid growth for the foreseeable future.

Here are of their upcoming projects in their Upstream segment.

Here are some of their Energy, Chemical, & Specialty Products projects.

And lastly, Low Carbon Solutions projects.

In Q3, aside from Energy, profits fell in each of their segments. Energy's performance was driven by higher volumes due to increased refinery output and projects.

Upstream, their largest, saw profits fall due to lower realizations and higher expenses.

Both the Chemical & Specialty Products segments were impacted by higher costs, which also impacted margins.

But management expects improvements to be driven by cost-savings initiatives, growth from current and future projects, improved efficiencies, and technological innovation.

Recent Dividend Increase

In early November, Exxon Mobil raised its dividend 4% to $1.03. This was a similar increase to last year, when the company increased the dividend by 4%. While they had exhibited strong cash flow growth despite volatility in their segments, some investors may have been disappointed in the 4% increase.

Management addressed why the dividend growth rate hasn't been more competitive vs the broader market during Q3:

We look at our overall dividend growth rate, and we constantly think about sustainability, right? We think about competitiveness, we think about growth. And when we measure it on those 3 pretty important qualitative factors, we feel pretty happy with where we've ended up.

The increase marked 43 consecutive years of dividend increases. XOM also has a solid dividend yield above 3% currently. Despite cash from operations declining year-over-year from $42.8 billion to $39.3 billion, the company only paid $12.9 billion in dividends through the first 3 quarters.

Furthermore, management raised their cash flow & earnings outlook through 2030. This should instill some confidence in their dividend sustainability going forward. And could also see management conduct slightly higher dividend raises in the 6% to 8% range going forward.

Because of XOM's strong business, projects, and bright outlook due to Venezuela's heavier crude vs America's lighter crude, I have no doubt that Exxon Mobil will join the Dividend King's list in the next 7 years.

Buybacks & Balance Sheet

One reason I believe XOM's management is more conservative on their dividend growth is that the company prefers to buyback shares, returning capital to shareholders this way.

During the quarter, XOM returned $9.4 billion. Of this, $5.1 billion was share repurchases. And for the fiscal year, management expects to repurchase $20 billion, slightly higher than the $19 billion in 2024.

As a result of XOM's strong balance sheet, this not only gives them flexibility for future acquisitions, it also enables them to buyback a substantial amount of shares. Which, going forward, could lead to solid earnings growth.

As far as their balance sheet goes, XOM's was in good shape with no near-term debt maturing, an A credit rating, and only $32.8 billion in long-term debt. This was minimal in comparison to their market cap of over $500 billion.

Liquidity was robust with $13.9 billion in cash & cash equivalents. Their balance sheet enables them to have the flexibility to make acquisitions, growing the business. In Q3, acquisitions totaled $2.4 billion. And going forward, I expect more in the years ahead.

Venezuela Could Unlock More Growth

Using their end-of-year earnings estimate, the company's forward P/E sits at 18x. This is higher than both their 5-year average and sector median. As evidenced by their quality, I believe XOM deserves to trade at a premium.

Despite their valuation, XOM's forward multiple still sits well-below peer Chevron's 22.29x at the time of writing. Shell (SHEL) and BP (BP) both trade significantly lower at 11.43x and 12.24x, respectively.

But as previously mentioned, with America's taking control of the country with the largest oil reserves, and the President mentioning the plan to have U.S. companies rebuild the country's energy infrastructure. If so, this could result in long-term growth for XOM and peers alike if successful.

If so, XOM's investment case is compelling as higher earnings growth will likely lead to higher dividend and share price growth. By 2028, the oil giant offers solid upside, according to FastGraphs. But if U.S. companies successfully reinvest in Venezuela, I suspect this could be even higher, closer to the $150 - $160 level.

Risks & Conclusion

Key risks for XOM include continued volatility in U.S. oil & gas prices. Moreover, geopolitical tensions will also play a part, as what will happen with cooperation from Venezuela remains unknown. Especially, since the President's military action against the country could face scrutiny.

However, with oil prices currently down, I believe Exxon's long-term investment case is compelling. JPMorgan (JPM) also named them as a top income investment idea for 2026.

While shares could remain volatile in the near to medium term, the invasion in Venezuela could unlock value for XOM, leading to strong growth going forward. This, their solid dividend yield above 3%, upside potential, and likelihood to see higher growth make them a compelling long-term investment.

data.title: Stride: Time To Double Down
data.description:
I Keep My Bullish Stance on LRN Stock

I initiated my coverage of Stride, Inc. (LRN) stock in early November, a few days after its massive drop after its dramatic drop in price following the Q1 data and commentary. While I can't say I'm pleased with how the stock has behaved since my call (it underperformed the market), I do like management's proactive approach and its attempts to shift the trend. The price action also seems to be stabilizing near the support zone as the market gets ready for LRN's upcoming Q2 data/commentary, which I believe has a high probability of triggering a strong mean reversion to the upside.

The current post-correction valuation of the stock has detached from reality, even when we assume meaningfully lower business growth ahead. LRN isn't a no-brainer GARP pick like most of the stocks I usually cover on Seeking Alpha, but it looks like a solid turnaround, buy-the-dip opportunity for those looking for a stock that can generate alpha this year (or over the next couple of years). I'm leaving my "Buy" on LRN with no changes.

Why Do I Think So?

The last time I looked at LRN, the firm's most recent report was from October 2025 (fiscal Q1 2026), and nothing has changed since then. In short, what triggered the massive sell-off, which the market has failed to rebuild, is the glitch in Stride's platform rollout. It cost them an estimated 10,000-15,000 enrollments. While painful, I think it wasn't fatal for LRN, because the total enrollments for the quarter arrived at 247,000 (+11.3% YoY). In fact, the actual Q1 data showed that demand for the firm's services remains at record highs - otherwise, I think LRN's student base wouldn't have grown by double digits.

Also, a classic operational disaster that ruins a business model usually leads to margin contractions, which is not the case for LRN. In Q1, the firm's adjusted operating income went up by 39% YoY (the non-GAAP EPS was up by the same %) amid the consolidated sales growth of just 13% YoY. Even assuming that Stride will need to spend extra on the platform's fixes, so far, its operating leverage has remained in solid shape. I believe that when the platform is fixed, this operating leverage should re-emerge and lead to stronger-than-expected EPS expansion in 2027 (and likely beyond). The current consensus data imply less than 8% EPS growth in FY2027, which looks like an underestimation given that LRN should be comping against a low base in FY2026 and that the platform fixes should roll through the year and meaningfully impact results in FY2027.

I assumed back in October that the management would likely roll out a clear roadmap to resolve the issues created within this fiscal year, and we should ultimately witness a return to normal retentions by FY2027. What we know is that the management has deliberately limited intake to ensure quality while they fix the platform:

we wanted to focus on running high-quality programs. And in some instances, the best approach to achieve that is to limit enrollment growth while we improve our execution

Source: LRN's Q1 earnings call, the CEO's comments

The trends in the past applications show strong volumes, and the demand for alternative education remains solid as more and more students choose flexible schedules. And there's also a massive need for career-focused learning. So, I believe that those 10,000-15,000 students who couldn't be added to the base this year will become a potential "market addition," so to speak, when the platform's UX is smoothed out.

As far as I see it, most market participants seem to focus just on the Q1 facts. Yes, the free government money that drove up Stride's financials over the past few years (LRN's revenues doubled throughout 2020-2024) is gone now. But the current consensus doesn't wait for the 2020-2024 levels of top-line growth. Anything slightly above the inflation rate should be enough to beat the Street's estimates:

On the call, however, the CEO, James Rhyu, clarified that the actual revenue impact was >3%, and if so, this bearish argument of "no funding leads to no growth" doesn't seem valid to me.

As long as LRN's profitability remains intact (so far, there's no indication to the contrary), management can use the current dip to the firm's advantage. They authorized a $500 million buyback program in early November, which is a lot for a ~$3 billion market cap firm, in my view.

The adjusted operating income for FY2026 is guided at a positive $475-500 million, and the guided FCFs are going to be positive for the next 3 quarters, which is what we heard on the Q1 call. Plus, the existing cash pile on the balance sheet appears to be enough for executing the existing buyback program in full.

As in years past, we expect to see positive cash flow for the next 3 quarters. We finished the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities of $749.6 million.

Source: The CEO's comments

Amid these buybacks, which can make LRN's EPS artificially higher in FY2026, we see a clear undervaluation - LRN trades at just 7.57x FY2027 earnings multiple. At the same time, the EV/Revenue multiple is close to ~1.6x:

According to FINRO's study, the median EV to revenue multiple among edtech firms sits at ~7.8x, while the median EV to funding is ~3.8x. We also have to consider growth rates, where LRN would lag most other edtech peers in terms of forward sales growth. That said, I doubt the gap is as wide as what current multiple comparisons imply.

Anyway, assuming that LRN achieves 10% growth in FY2027 EPS (vs. the current consensus of 7.95%) thanks to buybacks and general normalization, and it keeps ~1x in PEG ratio, the resulting price target would be $91.3.

New FY2027 EPS = $8.30 * 1.10 = $9.13

EPS * target P/E = PT

at PEG = 1x, PT = $9.13 * 10x = $91.3/share

Source: The author's calculations

This calculated PT implies an upside potential of almost 32% to the current stock price.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The biggest risk lies in execution. If the management's actions don't lead to any meaningful operational turnaround over the next weeks, and the platform fixes drag into 2H FY2026, it could impact the fall 2026 enrollment cycle and lead to continued opportunity cost and earnings contraction.

Another risk comes with regulatory issues. We know that Stride used to rely heavily on state-level public school funding, so assuming that schools cut ties as federal stimulus dries up, it can result in a natural demand drop for LRN's services/offerings.

Plus, LRN's valuation, when viewed through the lens of PEG, can actually look expensive because there's no margin of safety (i.e., the stock's forward PEG is ~0.95x now). And LRN's downtrend can easily continue if the market decides that the stock deserves a lower PEG amid all the operational issues that appeared in late calendar 2025.

The Bottom Line

I think that LRN's issues are technically fixable, and we should see the first signs of an operational turnaround over the next couple of quarters. The firm's business model proved that it can be profitable and that the top line can grow by double digits, even when funding is cut. I think that 2026 should be noticeably better in terms of the stock's performance compared to the second half of 2025. When we see real execution on the proposed normalization plan, and the market gets the necessary support from buybacks, the stock price usually rebounds ahead of the actual changes showing up in the financials. I see an upside of over 30% over the next 12 months, so I keep my "Buy" on LRN in place.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: SCOTUS Poised For Major Ruling On Tariffs
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Good morning! Here is the latest in trending:

Jobs Day: The latest non-farm payrolls report will likely be bolstered by seasonal hiring and the government restart. More details here.

Mega miner: Glencore (GLCNF) and Rio Tinto (RIO) have restarted talks over a possible merger almost a year after earlier talks collapsed.

EV trouble: General Motors (GM) is taking an additional $7.1B writedown tied to the decision to scale back its electric vehicle production and Chinese operations.

Dropping the hammer

It's not a given, but there's a good chance the Supreme Court could rule on the legality of President Trump's tariffs today after setting Friday as a decision day. SCOTUS has expedited the tariff case due to its outsized impact on businesses and the economy. At issue are Trump's across-the-board "reciprocal" tariffs on trading partners that are aimed at reducing the trade deficit, as well as the more targeted ones designed to counter fentanyl trafficking out of China, Mexico, and Canada.

Statutory interpretation: The tariffs were implemented under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act of 1977, which is usually used for economic sanctions and freezing assets. IEEPA powers also allow the president to "regulate importation," but the question here is whether tariffs fall under taxation, a power that the Constitution assigns to Congress, or can be viewed as a regulatory mechanism. Opponents of the tariffs have made a legal case that IEEPA authority only applies to things like quotas, licensing, and safety standards, while proponents have argued that the meaning is broader and includes any financial tool used to regulate trade in dealing with extraordinary threats.

"Will a strike down of tariffs require refunds? Likely but not guaranteed. The court should clarify if the decision applies retroactively to previous payments made or prospectively on a go-forward basis," Wells Fargo economists wrote in a research note. "It's also entirely possible that the court kicks the decision on refunds to the lower courts to decide, but even if the court decides Friday that refunds must be made nationwide, it may require individual importers/businesses to file a case to get the refund, lengthening the process. The bottom line is that refunds (if processed in full, somewhere around $130B) will take time to pass through the system."

Next moves: There is also a possibility that some tariffs may be struck down, while others are upheld, but even if the White House loses the entire case, there are several additional ways the administration can reimpose the tariffs (for now). "What is not in doubt is our ability to continue collecting tariffs at roughly the same level, in terms of overall revenues,” said Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who has already prepared a backup plan. "What is in doubt, and it's a real shame for the American people, is the president loses flexibility to use tariffs both for national security [and] negotiating leverage." (7 comments)

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

An Elite Team To Help You Generate An Income Portfolio With +9% Yield

SPYI Vs. JEPI: The Winner Has Finally Been Determined

Broadcom: Ready To Close Undervaluation Gap In 2026

Nvidia Pulls The Timeline Forward: Why Rubin Changes The AI Game

A Golden Buying Opportunity: 9% Yields The Market Is Grossly Mispricing

What else is happening...

Nvidia (NVDA) hires Google exec as its first marketing chief.

Iger in China: Disney (DIS) will continue to expand investment.

Decision on Fannie, Freddie IPOs likely in a month or two.

Toyota (TM) is the top EV seller in Japan for the first time.

Trump directs representatives to buy $200B in mortgage bonds.

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) enters federal pact for drug discounts.

Tilray Brands (TLRY) jumps as it posts record quarterly revenue.

Bessent discusses Fed chair search, interest rates and fiscal deficit.

Boeing (BA) CEO: More turnaround progress needed in 2026.

Iran cuts off the internet as Khamenei slams protesters and Trump.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +1.6%. Hong Kong +0.3%. China +0.9%. India -0.7%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.4%. Paris +0.8%. Frankfurt +0.3%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow flat. S&P +0.1%. Nasdaq +0.2%. Crude +0.7% to $58.19. Gold +0.5% to $4,481.30. Bitcoin +0.5% to $90,467.
Ten-year Treasury Yield to unchanged at 4.19%.

On The Calendar

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Omega Healthcare Investors: Why This Could Become My Favorite REIT
data.description:
For several months now, demographic dynamics have been catching my attention, especially companies operating in the aging population trend. Overall, it is a very interesting trend if we consider skilled nursing facilities.

There are some listed companies that are interesting at first glance, such as Ensign Group (ENSG) and PACS Group (PACS), which have market caps of $10.3 billion and $6.4 billion, respectively. But even though there is potential, and I need to investigate them further to see if they are a good case for investment, a preliminary analysis already shows that the case is not as simple as believing in the secular trend. Margins are compressed, and there are players such as Brookdale Senior Living (BKD) that are operating at a loss and with a compressed EBIT margin.

At the same time, even though the growth these players are delivering is already attractive and the prospects are strong, the valuation is also already high. For example, ENSG, which is one of the largest and one of the benchmarks in the sector, is trading at 30x forward earnings.

But what changed my view was Omega Healthcare Investors (OHI). It is a real estate company that manages to expose itself to this trend, but with much less risk than an operator. The complexity is low compared to the players mentioned above, and yet the growth is compelling.

This is a REIT that I thought I would look at to see if it was interesting, but at last I would not add it to the portfolio because I prefer companies with a higher growth profile for my portfolio. Surprisingly, even though I have only been following it for a short time, I think it has the potential to make it onto my list of favorite REITs, as it combines a number of positive factors.

The Positive Factors of Omega

In addition to the lower complexity factor already mentioned, Omega has other positive points, which they summarize well in their presentation. Note, for example, that we are talking about a well-established REIT with a proven business model. The image below highlights some key points, such as high margins and a solid balance sheet, enabled by the triple net lease model, portfolio diversification among tenants, and long-term contracts.

Going deeper, we are talking about a portfolio with just over 1,000 facilities 88 operators, divided into senior housing (40%) and skilled nursing (60%). Geographically, there is also good diversification. It is worth mentioning that 18% of the portfolio comes from the United Kingdom and Jersey, which is an added diversification in a demographically attractive market with a strong currency. The remainder is well divided among the other US states, with Texas leading with 8.6%.

The average lease term is 9.5 years, and although there is some uncertainty around specific years, such as 2027, when 7.9% of contracts expire, confidence in a potential renewal is high. It is a good trend with a lot of growth potential in the coming decades, and this means that most operators intend not only to maintain operations and consequently their leases but also to expand.

In addition to the qualities of the business model, such as high margins, predictability, and ease of operation, we also need to mention a little more about the growth history and how it impacts confidence in trends.

Since 2009, the company has grown its gross investments at a CAGR of 12.7%, and for several years now (since 2019), it has been investing around ~$10 billion, which indicates that there is still room for portfolio expansion. The CAGR of revenue and EBITDA were basically the same, around 12.5%, but with some volatility in recent years.

This consistency in management makes it possible to believe that they will continue to capture this demand, which, in my view, is one of the main pillars of the case. We are talking about a population of 65 million aged 65+, which will exceed 80 million in 2040, representing over 22% of the population. Because of this, it is estimated, as can be seen in the image below, that demand will exceed supply as early as 2030, causing occupancy to exceed 100% in this scenario.

This implies several points. In addition to saying that Omega can continue to invest billions per year, increasing supply without suffering from any level of excess, having to lose profitability, or anything like that, we can even say that potentially, the yield, which is already high (9%+ for skilled nursing facilities), may become even higher in the coming decades, especially for real estate with geographical or structural advantages.

The icing on the cake is that Omega Healthcare Investors is trading at a very reasonable valuation. Remember I mentioned multiples of 30x earnings for some operators in this industry? Well, OHI is trading at 14.6x FFO, which in itself already allows for an attractive dividend yield (around 6%) and expectations of higher shareholder yields as the company continues to compound revenue and EBITDA.

More fairly, even when compared to other triple net lease REITs, I still find it a very attractive valuation. Realty Income (O), which is the leading REIT and is very exposed to retail, which is more macroeconomically sensitive, trades at a P/FFO of 13.6x. VICI Properties (VICI), another REIT that I really like but which ends up being much more uncertain than OHI given its tenants, which are riskier and more leveraged, and, in general, its industry, which is more dependent on gambling, trades at 11.7x FFO.

2 Risks of OHI

Despite my bullish stance, I need to highlight two main risks of the case, which are basically the only two points that bother me.

The first is competition. It turns out that the prospects for this market are so good that it could become a double-edged sword. If regulations become looser, making it easier to obtain permits to build this type of facility, it is possible that yields will fall, causing the expected return on the investments that OHI is making now to be lower. Similarly, in this scenario, there could be more competition, and consequently, a decrease in operators' margins, increasing OHI's risk, since many of these operators already operate with tiny margins.

But the main point, for me, is regulation, more specifically of Medicare and Medicaid. Today, the mix of operators is only 23% private payments, 27% Medicare, and 50% Medicaid. It is a very high dependency.

As much as I find it extremely unlikely that any government will end or greatly reduce the benefits of these two programs, it is quite challenging to know what these programs will look like in 10 years, or even 5, since we may have both positive triggers and changes in payment timing, transfer amounts, co-payments, etc. These are highly unpredictable factors, and since there are already operators with squeezed margins, we may see some decline in revenue in these scenarios.

The Bottom Line

Even with these uncertainties, I still believe that OHI is a good REIT and, above all, a good way to gain exposure to the secular trend of population aging in a more reliable way while still collecting a high yield. Balancing this with the risks and uncertainties related to regulation, my rating is a buy.

data.title: Trump's $1.5 Trillion Defense Budget Is A Huge Gift To My Portfolio
data.description:
President Donald Trump just called for a $1.5 trillion defense budget in fiscal year 2027 as a drive toward achieving a "dream military" that he hopes will deter potential adversaries’ aggression and keep the peace. This is an objectively enormous outlay in and of itself, but it also represents rapid spending growth for the U.S. military, as it is 50% higher than this year’s already record military budget and represents a near 75% increase from the defense budget in President Joe Biden’s final year in office.

Such a massive increase in government spending will likely be mostly funded through additional deficit spending and government borrowing. The consensus view is that massive increases in defense spending are a major boon to defense stocks like Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT), Northrop Grumman Corporation (NOC), L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX), General Dynamics Corporation (GD), Teledyne Technologies Incorporated (TDY), Textron Inc. (TXT), Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc. (HII), Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), and Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. (KTOS), and while this is certainly true, at the moment I do not have any exposure to pure defense stocks. Nonetheless, I believe that this development, if it becomes a reality, will be an enormous gift to my portfolio, which is currently heavily invested in hard assets. In this article, I will detail why.

Why a $1.5 Trillion Defense Budget Changes the Macro Picture

If the defense budget truly increases to $1.5 trillion in 2027, independent estimates suggest that this could mean an additional $5 to $5.7 trillion in total spending through 2035, including extra interest on the debt. The funding for this defense budget will likely partially come from additional tariff revenue, but the vast majority will come from additional government borrowing, which indicates persistently large and likely growing government deficits rather than a simple, short, one-time, debt-funded stimulus spending on the military. The impact that this will likely have on the market (SPY) is upward pressure on inflation and likely increased long-term interest rates, as the government will have to increase borrowing, thereby creating additional supply-demand imbalance in the bond markets (TLT), which will in turn bring up interest rates further to attract additional lenders to meet demand for more U.S. government debt.

This announcement also has major implications for what is going on in the world today because President Trump would not feel such urgency to raise deficit spending so aggressively on the military if it were not for the fact that they had considerable intelligence suggesting that major adversaries could be on the threshold of undertaking major military operations that need a significantly strengthened U.S. military to deter. Thus, the geopolitical risk factor indicated by this proposed spending increase is rising and needs to be considered when assessing various investments.

Perhaps the portion of my portfolio that will benefit the most from this development is my heavily overweight allocation to gold bullion leases and gold miners (GDX). This is because gold (GLD) tends to benefit from the combination of war, deficit spending, and inflation. In fact, it has already been on a tremendous run over the past two years due to a combination of de-dollarization in central banks and institutions around the world, in which they are dumping treasuries and dollars and buying gold instead, alongside persistent deficit spending by the U.S., Federal Reserve rate cuts, and simmering geopolitical tensions.

While all of those tailwinds remain firmly in place, President Trump’s announcement indicates that geopolitical tensions and risk, as well as deficit spending and inflation concerns, are all strengthening further, thereby boding very well for the future performance of my gold bullion lease and gold mining investments.

The Hidden Winners

However, it is not just my gold investments that I think will benefit from this announcement. I have an even larger allocation in my portfolio to energy-related infrastructure, including investments in midstream energy (AMLP), hydropower, regulated utilities (XLU), and nuclear energy. This has been pushed by President Trump alongside his near-simultaneous demand that defense companies rapidly increase the number of plants and the rate at which they produce weaponry. All of this bodes very well for energy infrastructure (UTG), whether it be new and/or enhanced shipyards, military bases, weapons manufacturing, or military-related AI data centers; all of these entities require a significant and reliable supply of energy.

Additionally, the growing threat of war between U.S. allies and major adversaries like Russia and China is increasing the desire among countries in Europe and U.S.-allied trading partners in Asia, like Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan, to turn to the U.S. for energy security. This boosts the U.S. LNG export industry, which is expected to see its capacity rise 60% by 2030. Should war arise, or at least the threat of an increase, that will only provide an additional tailwind for energy exports and midstream LNG export leaders like Energy Transfer LP (ET).

Meanwhile, hydro, nuclear, and domestic U.S. energy infrastructure will be in high demand due to the need for reliable baseload power rather than intermittent supply to fuel the defense industry, as well as data centers and U.S. military bases. Not only that, but the inflationary and de-dollarization trends that a significant increase in military spending will unleash will likely only further increase the value of critical real assets, including energy infrastructure, especially since energy has historically proven to be one of the biggest beneficiaries of inflation.

Another portion of my portfolio that I expect to benefit from Trump's proposed massive defense budget increase is my investment in government-leased real estate via Easterly Government Properties, Inc. (DEA). DEA is a REIT (VNQ) that invests in built-to-suit, mission-critical real estate properties for the U.S. government and defense entities, including defense contractors like NOC. As they stated on their latest earnings call:

[we have a] stated goal of reach a 15% government adjacent exposure...Our development pipeline remains very active with major projects progressing nicely. We continue to identify accretive opportunities that meet our standards for credit quality, mission alignment, and durable returns.

Since they can generally earn higher yields on leases to government-adjacent tenants, a ramp-up of demand for mission-critical weapons production plants could provide some very attractive investment opportunities for DEA moving forward. Thus, President Trump’s renewed push to increase weapons production and the creation of new weapons plants, as well as increased investment in the military, should be a tailwind for DEA. In addition to increasing opportunities for them to invest in new property developments, it will also increase the economic value of their existing properties that they own, thereby boosting the chances that they will not only be able to re-lease those properties upon expiration but also do so at attractive spreads.

Investor Takeaway

While defense contractors will likely be the biggest immediate beneficiaries of Trump’s enhanced defense spending, at the same time, his demand that they invest more in weapons production and allocate far fewer resources to buybacks and dividends is not music to the ears of dividend-focused investors like myself. While I have invested heavily in the sector in the past, including a very successful investment in GD and a moderately successful one in HII and PLTR, today I am not invested in defense stocks and instead am heavily overweight in infrastructure and precious metals, and also own shares in government and defense contractor landlord DEA.

As I discuss in this article, I believe that these investments will benefit significantly from Trump’s military buildup. As a result, I view this announcement as a major gift to my 7–8% yielding, low-beta portfolios that have delivered attractive long-term total return performance over their five-year history at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Camurus: Why It Has Become A Meaningful Position In Our Portfolio
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Protean Funds Scandinavia AB November 2025 Partner Letter.

Camurus (CAMRF) has become a meaningful position for us recently. Not because we suddenly felt an urge to compete with specialist biotech funds, but because the gap between what the business is and what the market believes it is has quietly widened to an interesting degree.

FluidCrystal, the company’s injectable depot technology, is the source of both the opportunity and the uncertainty. We’ve always been wary of platforms that promise to “work for everything” – they usually don’t – but over the past few years the technology has done something rare in biotech: it has behaved consistently across molecules, indications, and partners.

An expert network interview we read recently described it as “years ahead of legacy depots” in terms of dose flexibility and tolerability, particularly for peptides. Encouraging, but not something we base the whole thesis on.

The pipeline around it is broad, but not without potholes. CAM2029 is clinically validated but operates in small, noisy patient groups. The addiction portfolio has clear strategic fit but will need patience. The Semaglutide long-acting data were undeniably strong, although early deltas tend to fade in longer studies and the incretin space is fast-moving enough that today’s excitement can become tomorrow’s footnote. The Lilly partnership adds credibility, but large pharma tends to move on its own timelines: slow, incremental, opaque. We view it as a door worth having, not a revenue line worth modelling.

GEP-NET is the part investors dislike the most, and with reason. The delayed event accumulation has led to predictable speculation about underpowered trials and overly effective control arms. These interpretations are not impossible, but they are not the only explanation either. Slow-progressing tumours tend to do what they tend to do (hint: it’s in the name). Our base case is that the study eventually reads out as hoped; our risk case is that markets grow tired of waiting and require visible proof before rewarding the company again. That is the emotional reality of biotech: even a correct thesis can be wrong in timing.

It helps that the foundation is solid. Buvidal/Brixadi continues to take share in long-acting buprenorphine, a market still in the early innings of converting from daily dosing. Camurus keeps gaining ground in Europe, and key competitor Indivior’s (INDV) decision to withdraw from multiple markets is an extraordinary signal of competitive weakness. This is the part of the business we actually pay for today, and it continues to behave the way we want a core position to behave: steadily and without much drama.

Valuation reflects the scepticism. On roughly mid-teens EBIT multiples two years out, with north of 10% of market cap in net cash, the stock is valued as if nothing beyond Buvidal will work, and as if each pipeline read-out is more likely to disappoint than not. Some caution is warranted – there are several ways for the next 18 months to be messy – but the current pricing implies a level of pessimism we do not share.

The risk/reward is attractive not because the story is flawless, but because we are not being asked to pay for the parts of the story that might eventually matter.

Camurus will test our patience. The catalysts are distant, and the roster has some early investors with sizeable gains likely to be chunky sellers along the way. But there is a dependable engine underneath, a pristine balance sheet, a long-time CEO with skin in the game, and a series of shots on goal that – on a probability-adjusted basis – are more valuable than the market currently credits. We like these setups: where you pay for the business you can see and get a portfolio of chances you don’t have to overpay for.

If we end up being wrong, the core business gives us some protection. If we end up being right, the optionality should matter far more than today’s valuation suggests. Let’s go.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: New Year's Resolutions For 2026 - Investor Version
data.description:
Every January, it happens like clockwork: you drive by gym parking lots that look like a Taylor Swift concert. Go to the store, and the salad aisles are ransacked like there’s a lettuce shortage, and half of your coworkers suddenly start quoting Warren Buffett while buying stock in companies they can’t spell. You got it; it’s “New Year’s Resolution Season.” That time of the year when we all promise ourselves to lose weight, work out, and save money, all before Valentine’s Day.

But by February, reality shows up with a cold slap. Those treadmill you bought is now a clothes rack for the laundry, and your credit card bill looks like you confused “budgeting” with “blowout sale.” Oh, and that investing plan you laid out? It turned into a Coinbase account holding three meme coins, a YouTube guru playlist, and a browser permanently stuck on Reddit’s WallStreetBets.

So why do we do this to ourselves every year? We can blame history and human nature.

The Babylonians started the tradition of New Year’s resolutions by promising their gods they’d return borrowed tools. Not a bad idea, unless you’re the guy who lent out a plow in 1900 B.C. and never saw it again. Then the Romans made it official by swearing oaths to Janus, their god of beginnings. Janus had two faces—one for looking back at last year’s mess and one for pretending this year’s going to be different. That’s also where we get the name “January.” Fitting for a month built entirely on denial.

Back then, New Year’s resolutions were about crops and keeping your ox alive. Now it’s about washboard abs and beating the S&P 500 by following someone named “CryptoWolf69” on social media.

Why the obsession? Because being average feels like failure. New Year’s resolutions offer a psychological sugar rush by tricking your brain into thinking momentum is action. You say you’ll track your spending, invest consistently, and finally understand how options work. But three weeks later, you’re back to impulse-buying crypto at midnight while watching reruns of Shark Tank.

However, this is where the wheels fall off. New Year’s resolutions don’t fail because you’re weak; they fail because you built them on a foundation of hope, caffeine, and Instagram quotes. You set goals that sound great after two glasses of wine on New Year’s Eve. But you skip the parts that matter—routine, discipline, and not quitting after three bad days. You want the six-pack, but not the push-ups. You want the returns, but not the risk management.

It’s the same with investing.

You swear you’ll “invest for the long term." Unfortunately, the “long term” only exists until the market drops 5%, and suddenly you’re all cash, reading articles with headlines like “Is This the Big One,” and watching YouTube channels with individuals claiming the end has finally come. While you say retirement is a priority, you’ve never run a projection or calculated your savings rate. You make investment decisions based on TikTok, then act shocked when your portfolio resembles one that was managed by a teenager.

Most people don’t destroy their portfolios in one move. They do it slowly, by:

  • Building bad habits,
  • Assuming motivation will last, and
  • Confusing effort with consistency.

By the time they realize it’s not working, the damage is already done.

Short-term enthusiasm is not a plan. It’s an illusion.

If your investing goals are tied to the calendar instead of a disciplined process, you’re not managing money; you are chasing a feeling, and just like your unused gym membership, that approach ends in frustration. Every. Single. Time.

So, why do we continue to make bad investment decisions?

Why We Continue To Repeat Our Mistakes

Every year, Dalbar Research releases a report that reads like a horror story for investors. Different year, same conclusion: we are our own worst enemy.

The problem isn’t just a lack of money. It’s what goes on between your ears. Dalbar outlined nine classic investing behaviors that sabotage your returns faster than you can say “buy the dip.”

  • Loss Aversion – You fear losing money so much that you sell everything… right before the rebound.
  • Narrow Framing – You obsess over one stock while ignoring the rest of your portfolio, slowly imploding.
  • Anchoring – You keep waiting for a stock to “get back to even,” like it owes you something.
  • Mental Accounting – You treat your retirement fund and crypto wallet like two different worlds, even though both are on fire.
  • Lack of Diversification – You think owning five tech stocks is a “balanced strategy.”
  • Herding – You invest because everyone else is. It ends exactly how you’d expect.
  • Regret Aversion – You don’t act because you’re still haunted by the time you sold Apple in 2012.
  • Media Response – You react to financial news like it’s a fire drill, even when it’s just smoke.
  • Optimism Bias – You think every investment will “bounce back.” Even the one currently under SEC investigation.

The worst offenders are herding and loss aversion. This is because investors jump in when markets are euphoric but then panic-sell during every correction. It’s the financial version of eating an entire pizza and then blaming your scale. However, we keep repeating these mistakes because markets can play tricks with your mind. The higher they go, the more you believe the rally is permanent. The lower they drop, the more you’re convinced it’ll never recover. You buy high, sell low, and wonder why your account never grows.

This is why you need a different kind of resolution, one built for the reality of investor behavior, not your fantasy of becoming the next Warren Buffett overnight.

Investor Resolutions for 2026 (That Might Actually Work)

Let’s be honest: emotions ruin portfolios. So in 2026, skip the vague promises and start with specific rules that stand a chance against your worst instincts. Here’s a better resolution list, built for real investors, not fantasy league traders:

In 2026, I will (or at least attempt to):

  • Stick with what’s working and cut what’s not. No more “hoping it turns around.”
  • Respect the trend. Fighting it is how you get broke.
  • Be bullish or bearish—but not greedy. Hogs get slaughtered.
  • Accept that paying taxes means I made money. That’s a good thing.
  • Buy in stages, use limit orders, and stop chasing like it’s Black Friday.
  • Hunt for value, not train wrecks with a PR team.
  • Diversify because bad stuff always happens somewhere.
  • Set stop-losses. Use them and don’t argue with them.
  • Actually do the research before you click “buy.”
  • Don’t panic when markets fall. Breathe. Then double-check your plan.
  • Treat cash like a position, not an insult.
  • Expect market corrections. Act like an adult when they show up.
  • Be ready to admit when you are wrong, rather than doubling down due to your ego.
  • Leave “hope” out of your investment thesis.
  • Stay flexible. Stubbornness isn’t a strategy.
  • Be patient. Results take time, not adrenaline.
  • Turn off the TV, log off TikTok, and spend more time with data than influencers.

I try to follow this list every year, and, like every year, I screw up a few items. That’s okay. The goal isn’t to be perfect; it’s to make fewer mistakes than last year because investing success doesn’t come from reading motivational quotes or watching market TikToks at midnight.

Like fitness, you don’t get results because you “bought” a gym membership. You get them because you showed up when it was hard. Investing is the same. There are no shortcuts and no magic formulas. There are only basic rules, boring discipline, and the consistency to do them longer than the guy next to you.

Want to be a better investor? Then keep your resolutions, even when the market gives you every reason not to.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Tariffs On Decision Watch
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Supreme Court may decide fate of Trump's tariffs on Friday. (00:22) Alibaba (BABA) shares jump on potential China approval of Nvidia (NVDA) H200 imports. (01:33) Bob Iger: Disney (DIS) will continue to expand its investment in China. (02:31)

This is an abridged transcript.

The Supreme Court is expected to issue rulings today, which could possibly include one on the legality of tariffs that President Trump imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA).

According to the court's website, it could release decisions on argued cases on Friday. While it does not disclose which decisions it intends to issue ahead of time, the court's expedited handling of the tariff case indicates that a ruling is a possibility.

Trump invoked the IEEPA last February to impose reciprocal tariffs on countries to address the national emergency posed by "large and persistent" trade deficits.

The move was legally challenged, with lower courts ruling that the use of IEEPA to impose tariffs exceeded the president's authority.

Appeals by the Trump administration brought the case to the Supreme Court.

"PRAY THAT THE UNITED STATES SUPREME COURT ALLOWS OUR COUNTRY TO CONTINUE ITS UNPRECEDENTED MARCH TOWARD UNPARALLELED GREATNESS!," Trump posted on Truth Social on Tuesday, while thanking tariffs for pushing U.S. markets to record highs.

Prediction markets Kalshi and Polymarket each give a 25% chance of the Supreme Court ruling in favor of Trump's tariffs.

If the Supreme Court rules that the IEEPA-based tariffs are unlawful, the U.S. government may have to refund more than $133.5B in duties to importers, data from the U.S. Customs and Border Protection showed.

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) saw its shares rally as much as 3.1% in Hong Kong—marking its strongest intraday performance since late 2024.

This was triggered by a Bloomberg report stating that Beijing is preparing to approve the import of Nvidia’s H200 AI chips for commercial use as early as this quarter.

The news sparked a sector-wide rally, with other Chinese AI leaders like Kuaishou Technology (KUASF) and JD.com (JD) each gaining over 4%.

According to sources, Alibaba has made a confidential inquiry about purchasing more than 200,000 units of the H200 to support its large language Models (LLMs) and compete with American competitors like OpenAI.

The current optimism stems from a policy shift in December 2025, where the Trump administration authorized the export of H200 chips to China, provided a 25% surcharge is paid to the U.S. government.

Disney (DIS) CEO Bob Iger met with Chinese Vice Premier Ding Xuexiang in Beijing on Friday and said the entertainment conglomerate will continue to expand its investment in China.

Iger said Disney (DIS) is confident in China's development and will better promote exchanges and cooperation between the U.S. and China, state-run Xinhua News Agency reported.

Ding called on enterprises from all countries, including Disney (DIS), to continue investing and deepening their presence in China.

Notably, Beijing only allows a limited number of foreign films into the country annually, and Hollywood films are capped at 10 a year.

Disney (DIS) opened Shanghai Disneyland in 2016, and Iger's visit to China may fuel speculation that the company could be weighing opening another theme park in the country.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Trump's tilt towards crypto remains strategic for midterm elections, says Cathie Wood

GM takes an additional $7.1B charge tied to reduced EV capacity, Chinese business

TSMC December revenue rises 20.4% year over year, slips from November

Catalyst watch:

  • The three-day Silicon Valley Auto Show will begin. Toyota (TM), Cadillac (GM), Lucid (LCID), and Volkswagen (VWAGY) are all expected to show off 2026 models. Tesla (TSLA) will also be making a rare auto show appearance with its Cybertruck.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.3% at $57/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.6% at $90,000. Gold is down 0.1% at $4,473.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.3% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Tilray Brands (TLRY) +8% - Shares rose after reporting FQ2 2026 results that topped revenue expectations, marking its highest quarterly revenue on record.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Employment Situation

  • 8:30 am Housing Starts and Permits

  • 10:00 am Minneapolis Federal Reserve Bank President Neel Kashkari gives welcome remarks and participates in a fireside chat before the virtual 2026 Regional Economic Conditions Conference.

  • 1:35 pm Richmond Federal Reserve Bank President Tom Barkin will speak at the Maryland Bankers Association

data.title: AI Ambitions, Rising Debt: What 2026 Means For Bond Investors
data.description:
A wave of spending to finance investments in Artificial Intelligence is expected to fuel corporate debt issuance to new heights in 2026. Rachana Bhat, Vice President and Director, Investment Grade Credit Portfolio Management, at TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss the opportunities, what could change in the year ahead, and the main risks to watch.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Major spending on artificial intelligence drove corporate debt sales to near-record levels in 2025. So the question becomes, will that trend continue this year? And what does it mean for investors? Joining us now to discuss is Rachana Bhat, VP and director for investment-grade credit portfolio management with TD Asset Management. Rachana, great to have you back on the show.

Rachana Bhat: Great to have you back, and happy New Year.

Greg Bonnell: Happy New Year to you, too. We had an interesting 2025. We probably have an interesting 2026 ahead of us. Let's start with a little report card about how 2025 played out, how you would describe the year that just wrapped up.

Rachana Bhat: Absolutely. And when I think about 2025 and investment-grade corporate credit market, the phrase that really stands out to me is exceptional resilience. And that's despite the fact that going into 2025, the spread--, which is the extra compensation you earn for owning corporate bonds instead of government bonds-- that compensation was already pretty low. So you would expect that spreads will be a little more sensitive to any sort of negative shocks.

And by the way, we got quite a lot of those through the year-- whether that was tariffs, whether that was all the back-and-forth on it, credit wobbles, the labor market data that had started to show up in the second half. Credit really stayed resilient, if you will, versus even the other risk assets-- call it equities. As a portfolio manager, you would want to lean underweight at those levels because they are historically very low. But if you did that, it would have been a mistake for 2025 because, again, there was a lot of resiliency in the market. And that's what we saw.

Greg Bonnell: So we see that resilience. But now when you talk about that extra income you earn from the corporate bonds, those credit spreads, right now they're even lower than they were in 2025. Yet, you still say corporate bonds are an interesting place to be taking a look. And walk me through that.

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, maybe first I'll talk to what created that resiliency. And if we can bring up the chart that I brought here with me, the big, big reason for that resiliency has been-- if you look at this chart-- it's been those yields. Look at the chart and you'll see that yields are historically still very elevated. So imagine earning 4-5% in almost guaranteed returns, because this is a very strong asset class-- or safe asset class, if you will.

So for the safe asset class, you're earning 4-5% in yields. And that was a pretty attractive value proposition for investors. This was at the same time in 2025 where the economic backdrop was quite friendly. It was better than feared, despite those tariffs. You had corporate fundamentals looking strong. You had earnings coming out strong. So that demand was on fire from many, many kinds of investors.

You saw some of the strongest inflows in the asset class in 2025. And that's exactly what drove this resiliency. But the other interesting observation-- and I'm still sticking to 2025, and we'll get to 2026-- the other interesting observation about 2025 was, credit was resilient. But if you look at US investment-grade spreads, how much did they fall? Spreads falling is good for price. It's inversely correlated to price performance. So when spreads fall, it's a good thing. But those spreads only fell five basis points-- so very small in the context of S&P 500, which was up 16%.

So you should question, why did that happen? Well, that happened because we started from a more constrained valuation base in 2025. So as you think about credit spreads and what can drive credit spreads going forward, it's usually three things.

Fundamentals-- so macro and micro. If that's OK, then the corporate bond markets focus on technicals, which is supply and demand. What's happening to bond supply and demand day in and day out? And the third is valuations. And remember, this is a mean-reverting asset class, which is, the spreads gravitate towards long-term averages. It's unlike equities, which is a trending market.

So as we head into 2026, that valuation is now a lot more constrained, as you mentioned, than it was in 2025. But corporate markets are still the way to go in 2026. You just need to pick the right spots in the credit market.

Greg Bonnell: Let's talk about some of those spots, though, where obviously a big spot last year was artificial intelligence-- a lot of money being raised out there, a lot of supply. Even for this year, some of the projections I've seen are very, very big in terms of how much supply could come on the market. Are you concerned about that at all?

Rachana Bhat: Yeah, so if now we think about the 2026 backdrop for what our expectation is for 2026, as I mentioned, the spreads right now are quite constrained. The valuation is very constrained going into 2026. So, it's still not the reason why you would be underweight credit-- and we'll build the thesis on it and then we'll talk about AI as well.

So, when you think about 2026, let's go through those three factors-- fundamentals, technicals, and valuation. So, if you look at fundamentals right now-- or for 2026-- we can argue, broadly speaking, the economic, corporate, and the rates backdrop looks reasonably supportive for at least the first half of 2026. Corporate balance sheets are in exceptional and very solid position coming into 2026.

Now, the issue actually comes from the fact-- you mentioned AI-- the supply equation, the technicals. Now, if you look at this chart again here for a moment, you'll see that the spreads have actually-- sorry, the yields have actually fallen from where they were at the start of 2025. This means that this backdrop is very friendly for the companies to be issuing more bonds. They're leaning into this constructive backdrop and saying, well, we can now pursue more M&A, which we haven't been able to do for the last two, three years because of the uncertainty. Also, the AI CapEx-- that's transformational.

So, all of this supply can start to show up and continue to be a headwind for the bond market. But at the same time, your demand is coming off of the peaks because, again, the resiliency was driven by the yields, and those yields have fallen a little bit.

Greg Bonnell: So it sounds like it's manageable. So it's manageable?

Rachana Bhat: Correct. So when you think about, again, the 2026 backdrop, we are expecting modest widening from how we think about 2026. But even with that widening, for context, the extra income you earn from corporate bonds can actually absorb some of that widening. And it's a reasonable base case, if you will, for us that there could be some widening because of that technical headwind and how the valuations are a lot more constrained. And it could bring some sector dispersion. It could be the tech sector, which we've seen a little bit over the last year as well. And it could be even consumer sector or health care, which is pursuing more M&A and we see a lot more supply.

But again, we still don't see risk on the fundamental side because this releveraging, if you will, is happening from a point of strength. Balance sheets, as I mentioned, are in a great place. So, a meaningful fundamental problem is unlikely just yet. So it wouldn't be a major driver of spreads for this year. But it's something to watch.

Greg Bonnell: Unlikely just yet, but there's always risk. So when you're doing that risk exercise, what are you looking at?

Rachana Bhat: So when you look at the risk exercise, obviously the biggest, biggest theme right now is AI. And that's because all the spending that's happening towards AI-- think about all the CapEx that's going in. It's actually creating major fundamental structural shifts in how the businesses will operate, in how the labor market will shape in the years that we see ahead, as well as the new industries that get created from this AI boom.

But the other problem is the negative as well. And there could be negative on the fundamental side. But there's also a lot of financing that is needed for this AI, call it investment cycle. And that funding needs to be spread across the capital-- the entire capital market will need to participate in this funding, which means the tentacles are spreading across different markets. And that, to me, is very important to be focusing on. It's both aspects.

So AI is going to be a very, very important theme that markets focus on in the years to come, which means there'll be a lot of volatility around AI-related headlines, good or bad. And you want to make sure you're doing the homework to be on the good side of it, not the bad side, because there will be losers and winners despite whether it's a boom or bust AI cycle. And you need resources for that.

So, if I think about the risks at the moment, the upside risk-- again, because AI is such an important theme, it will come from the fact that upside is-- let's call it price upside for corporate bonds. If AI abilities outpace expectations, if that supply expectations that we went into this year, expecting that there'll be so much supply and market investors are positioned for it-- what if we don't get that supply? What if it underwhelms? That's a positive for spreads as well, because again, the technical backdrop, the demand backdrop, has come off of the highs but it's still pretty strong.

And the third is, the positive that I see is, if you have just very strong growth backdrop, natural disinflation, AI is steady or doing well-- the theme around that. The downside, obviously, is-- again, related to AI-- what if you see some headlines that are negative, that are bad, related to data center build-outs? The second thing is, what if that supply is to the upside? What if there's a lot more that needs to be funded which is not baked in just yet? And the third is inflation. So, what if the inflation risks come back?

So these are the three things that we believe could drive spreads to be-- our base case, as I mentioned, 10-15 basis points wider spreads from here. Canada is a wild card with USMCA. We have to see how that shapes up. Also, Venezuelan oil is not really great for Canada. So, some things to think about for Canada with where valuations are. But broadly speaking, I think 10-15 basis points is a reasonable base case for widening. And, as I mentioned, there are some upside and downside risks to that base case.

Original Post

data.title: 2026 China Outlook: Galloping Into The Year Of The Horse
data.description:
Introduction

We remain constructive on China stocks after they delivered another impressive year of performance in 2025, outpacing US and global benchmarks. The release of DeepSeek's hyper-efficient large language model (LLM) in January coincided with a significant re-rating10 of China's internet and technology stocks. However, we believe the re-rating actually began in January 2024 after a derivative-linked selloff may have marked a potential bottom for China's internet firms' valuations.

Despite strong performance in 2025, KWEB remains significantly below its all-time high.

Many investors remain unaware of the strong performance of Chinese stocks due, in part, to Western media bias. The rally in 2025 was broad, and the below China ETFs delivered strong total returns.

We believe several headwinds may swing to tailwinds in 2026, allowing for a continued re-rating of China stocks.

Potential Tailwinds For 2026:

  • The 15th Five-Year Plan emphasizes domestic consumption and technology self-reliance
  • Raising domestic consumption seeks to address trade imbalance concerns from both the United States and the European Union.
  • An increase in domestic consumption could improve the fundamentals of E-Commerce companies and end the heightened competition for the same high-income, urban wallets that has weighed on margins.
  • President Trump’s April 2026 trip to China is an opportunity to broaden the US-China trade truce, which would help alter the geopolitical and media narrative.
  • Anti-involution could be widely implemented beyond solar, which could help trigger inflation and improve corporate profit margins broadly.

Although these factors may support China's equity market, policy error is always a risk, and corporates and consumers should play a leading role in driving growth. Let’s take a deeper dive into these topics.

The 15th Five-Year Plan: Technology Innovation & Sustainable Growth

A key component of our original thesis for the creation of KraneShares was investing alongside government policy. We have seen firsthand the power of the government’s economic and social roadmap, as businesses often completely reorient themselves every five years to the sectors highlighted in each Five-Year Plan (FYP). Understanding what these plans entail is crucial for investing and doing business in China. In 2026, we will find out what the government's priorities are for the next five years through the release of the 15th FYP.

The 15th FYP will need to be approved at the National People’s Congress, which is expected to take place in March 2026. However, thanks to a draft released by the Central Committee in October, we already have a good idea of what to expect.

The goals from the draft are as follows:

  • Achieve high-level self-reliance in science and technology, and lead the development of new quality productive forces.
  • Build a strong domestic market and accelerate the construction of a new development pattern.
  • Expand the nation's artificial intelligence (AI) ecosystem and enhance its governance.
  • Promote healthy, rather than destructive, competition.
  • Stimulate the economy through coordinated fiscal and monetary policy.

Based on press releases from subsequent meetings between President Xi and the State Council, quantifiable goals could include the following:

  • Fiscal Deficit: 4% of gross domestic product (GDP)
  • Special Central Government Bond & Local Government Bond Issuance Target: RMB 4.4 trillion ($0.6 trillion)
  • Reserve Requirement Ratio (RRR): -0.5% cut to release RMB 1 trillion in liquidity (long-term)
  • 7-Day Reverse Repo Rate: -0.1% cut

The Central Economic Work Conference (CEWC) is an annual economic meeting where President Xi and the State Council, i.e., the highest echelons of China's government (basically the President and his cabinet), face off with the Central Committee to develop an economic game plan for the coming year. This year, attendees acknowledged economic challenges, which is a good sign. Compared to the draft FYP, the CEWC placed more of an emphasis on supporting domestic consumption growth.

Below, we have translated and paraphrased key goals that came out of the CEWC in 2025:

  • Strengthening domestic demand
  • Taking special actions to boost domestic consumption
  • Adherence to innovation-driven development and the acceleration of the cultivation and expansion of new growth drivers.
  • Deepening the expansion of the "Artificial Intelligence +" policy while improving the governance thereof.
  • Reforming policies to support high-quality development while further rectifying destructive competition.
  • Adherence to opening up and the promotion of "win-win" cooperation in various fields
  • Meeting the needs of both urban and rural households
  • Reducing unemployment
  • Strengthening clean energy and emissions reduction policies.
  • Stabilizing the real estate market by implementing city-specific policies to optimize the housing supply, including the acquisition of some existing commercial housing for use as affordable housing.

Finally, in December, President Xi published an article titled Expanding Domestic Demand Is a Strategic Move. The article states that “the expansion of domestic demand is related to both economic stability and economic security” and that domestic demand will be supported by “promoting employment, improving social security" among other measures.

The proposed 15th FYP indicates that technological innovation and broad economic expansion are key priorities. Meanwhile, behind the scenes, particular attention is being paid to domestic consumption growth by the highest levels of government.

US-China Relations: Trump's Turn

We believe markets are underestimating President Trump’s desire to reset US-China relations. We are optimistic that trade and national security measures already implemented or underway could give the Administration confidence to work on better relations in the long term. These measures include the onshoring of auto manufacturing and other critical industries, as well as chip export restrictions.

We believe that President Trump could extend the trade "truce" established with President Xi during their meeting in South Korea earlier this year. While we could see more hawkish bills coming out of Congress, such as the BIOSECURE Act1, we believe these are unlikely to seriously derail the White House's efforts.

Although China's currency, the renminbi (CNY), initially weakened after Trump's tariff announcements in April, the currency rose steadily through the end of 2025.2 We believe this indicates that currency markets are no longer pricing in tariff-driven disruptions.

Allocation: Dollar Weakness Supports Flow To China Stocks

Despite compelling valuations on an absolute and relative basis, we believe a negative media narrative, driven by geopolitics, has weighed on US investor sentiment towards China. European investors, on the other hand, did not hesitate to increase their allocations to China in 2025, possibly to offset declines in the US dollar that threatened to erode gains in US equities.11

In 2025, the weakness of the US dollar compared to the Euro, Swiss Franc, and Swedish Krona in 2025 has deterred new equity investments in the United States. In Euro terms, the S&P 500 Index returned less than 5% in 2025.

Meanwhile, European flows to China ETFs were far stronger than in the US.

Gains in the US dollar exchange rate for the renminbi tend to lend themselves to positive performance in the equity prices of companies with revenues denominated in renminbi, though the currency is far from the only factor at play. In 2025, KWEB followed the renminbi higher up until October. We believe the correlation was broken after investors became concerned that AI enthusiasm was leading to unjustified valuations globally. In 2026, markets could continue to re-rate KWEB companies based on the rise in the dollar value of their revenues, as indicated by the renminbi exchange rate.

Anti-Involution: Addressing Excessive Competition To Trigger Inflation & Domestic Consumption Growth

A significant economic issue in recent years for China has been overcapacity and excessive competition. The consequences are wide-ranging, from slim profit margins for local companies, a deflationary spiral, and admonishments from the US and EU for the excess supply of goods being unloaded on their markets.

Efforts to resolve this problem are not new. China first implemented an annual production cap of 45 million tons of aluminum per year starting in 2017.7 However, these issues have become more acute in recent years, under pressure from protectionism globally and the sheer scale of China's manufacturing base today.

In a July speech, President Xi mentioned overcapacity directly.6 In short order, the State Administration of Market Regulation (SAMR) said that it would work with corporations to curb excess competition in photovoltaics.4 This may have helped the price of polysilicon, a key input in solar panels, increased significantly in 2025, based on futures markets.

Solar panel production is dominated by China-based companies, including Tongwei and LONGi Green Energy. Despite being global leaders with historically strong top-line revenue growth, these companies have shown little to no net income in the past five quarters.

The next several quarters will determine if the companies can continue to reduce losses. In December, an industry association called Guanghe Qiancheng Technology Co., Ltd. (GQT) was organized by nine solar panel companies, at the request of China's ministers. GQT said it will “buy and shut down more than 1 million tons of capacity” in 2026.6

However, such direct and decisive action, in response to pleas from government officials, has yet to be seen in other industries. The Ministry of Industry & Information Technology (MIIT) has discouraged E-Commerce companies from continuing to drive down their own margins to compete in instant delivery in urban areas. Meanwhile, SAMR is looking to protect merchants from being undercut by platforms and has required that platforms adhere to reasonable price floors. However, E-Commerce platforms have yet to organize collectively in the same fashion as solar panel manufacturers.9

But, these agencies' comments on instant commerce have been echoed by the Mainland press, heightening urgency. On July 9th, the People’s Daily published an article on a “Takeaway War”, stating that “there is no winner in a price war, only innovation can bring the future”. The article notes that although “ultra-low prices may benefit consumers,” they can also cause irrational consumption, dilute profits per customer, and "make delivery drivers exhausted."8

We believe solving the involution issue could help trigger inflation, induce consumption, and improve the profitability of corporations, all of which could potentially benefit China's equity market.

Conclusion

2025 was a strong year for Chinese equities, driven by improved sentiment, especially around growth and technology sectors. Many investors reallocated during the year, though some continue to sit on the sidelines, especially US investors sensitive to geopolitical headlines. We are hopeful that 2026 could see continued strong performance in China's equity market, supported by the 15th Five-Year Plan's focus on technological self-reliance, anti-involution policies improving corporate profits, and increased consumer spending. Meanwhile, we believe the Trump Administration will seek progress on resetting diplomatic relations with China, reducing headline risks and allowing some US investors to re-allocate.

The preceding discussion includes forward-looking statements. Forward-looking statements are based on an assessment of market conditions at a certain point in time, and the views they express are subject to change without notice. Holdings are subject to change.

Index Definitions:

MSCI Emerging Markets Index: The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free-float weighted equity index that captures large and mid cap representation across Emerging Market (EM) countries. The index covers approximately 85% of the free-float adjusted market capitalization in each country. The index was launched on January 1, 2001.

S&P 500 Index: An American stock market index based on the market capitalizations of 500 large companies having common stock listed on the NYSE or NASDAQ. The index was launched on March 4, 1957.

Nasdaq 100 Index: The Nasdaq-100 Index tracks 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on the NASDAQ stock market, including major tech, retail, and healthcare firms, and was launched on January 31, 1985, as a benchmark for innovation-driven growth, not including financial firms.

MSCI All Country World Index (ACWI) ex USA: The MSCI ACWI ex USA Index captures large and mid cap representation across 22 of 23 Developed Markets (DM) countries (excluding the US) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries*. With 1,973 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global equity opportunity set outside the US. The index was launched on January 1, 2001.

Term Definitions:

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): The total dollar value of everything a country makes (goods like cars, services like haircuts) in a specific time, usually a year, showing how big its economy is and how healthy it's doing.

Revenue: The total amount of money that a company makes from selling goods and/or services within a specific period of time, before the deduction of any expenses.

Net Income: The amount of money that is left over from a company's revenue after taking into account the expenses required to produce said revenue.

Photovoltaics: The branch of technology concerned with the production of electric current at the junction of two substances. In this report, it refers to solar panels specifically.

ETF Net Flow: The money invested in a category of exchange-traded funds (ETFs) during a certain period of time, measured as buys subtracted from sell.

USD per CNY Exchange Rate: The amount of US dollars in one unit of the renminbi (CNY).

The Renminbi (CNY): The official currency of the People's Republic of China (PRC).

Polysilicon Futures: Polysilicon futures are standardized exchange‑traded contracts that obligate the buyer and seller to transact a specified quantity of polysilicon (a high‑purity form of silicon used mainly in solar cells and semiconductors) at a predetermined price on a future date. They are primarily used by producers, solar‑module makers, and traders to hedge against or speculate on volatility in polysilicon spot prices along the photovoltaic supply chain.
